findings.what: No drill covers the Verifiable Presentation — how the holder actually presents a VC to the verifier (signed with a verifier nonce for replay protection), which is the holder-side mechanism the owned sub-part 7c ('who are the key roles ... and what do they do') asks about.
findings.evidence: IAM - JWT.txt PAGE 43: 'Signed by the holder with a nonce from the verifier so that it can't be replayed. The holder can only present the entire credential as a verifiable presentation even if some data attributes are not needed for the verification.' Drills L10-7c-3/7c-4 stop at 'Holder ... stores the credential in a digital wallet' / 'presents it to a verifier' without naming the Verifiable Presentation or its anti-replay nonce. Absent from drills L10-7c-1..7c-7.
findings.fix: Add one drill: holder packages the VC into a Verifiable Presentation, signs it with a nonce supplied by the verifier so it cannot be replayed; this is the holder's active role and the last step of the trust triangle (issuer issues VC -> holder holds in wallet -> holder presents VP -> verifier checks issuer signature). Grounded in IAM - JWT.txt PAGE 43.

findings.what: Drill 7c-4 asserts the verifier 'does not have to contact the issuer at verification time' as an unqualified rule; the lecture's own VC example shows a credentialStatus/revocation list, so for revocation the verifier may still consult an issuer-published status endpoint.
findings.evidence: IAM - JWT.txt PAGE 36: '"credentialStatus": { "id": "https://socrates-university.authOlab.com/vcs/credential/status/14", "type": "CredentialStatusList2017" }', and PAGE 33 lists 'the revocation mechanism' as credential metadata. The drill's modelAnswer omits revocation status checking entirely.
findings.fix: Qualify 7c-4: signature/authenticity verification is offline against the issuer's public key, but revocation/validity may require checking the issuer-published credentialStatus list (W3C status list shown on PAGE 36). Keeps the core 'trust flows from issuer signature' point while not overstating full offline verification.

coverageVerdict: Drills collectively cover all three owned sub-parts well: 7a (JWT vs session ID, statelessness, scaling, revocation) and 7b (header alg/typ, three claim classes, all seven registered claims iss/sub/aud/exp/nbf/iat/jti, signature computation and role, JWS/JWE, alg=none attack) are accurate against the slides and RFC 7519; 7c covers VC components, roles, encoding (JWT-VC vs JSON-LD), and JSON-LD. The most exam-critical gap is the missing Verifiable Presentation step (holder->verifier with replay-protecting nonce), the one holder-side mechanism shown on the deck's final VC diagram that no drill delivers.
